schools.summary: King Arad attacking Israel and taking captives: 'and when king Arad the Canaanite, which dwelt in the south, heard tell that Israel came by the way of the spies; then he fought against Israel, and took some of them prisoners' — the Canaanite king of Arad, in the south, hearing of Israel's approach, attacked and took some captive (the king Arad the Canaanite, which dwelt in the south, the Canaanite foe in the Negeb, the heard tell that Israel came by the way of the spies, the route by which Israel came, the fought against Israel, and took some of them prisoners, the assault, the captives taken, the figure, the first onset of the Canaanite against Israel as they neared the land, the enemy attacking the pilgrim people, the trial of the way, the foe taking some captive, the truth that the people of God meet opposition as they near the inheritance, the enemy assailing them, the assault that would drive Israel to vow themselves wholly to God, vv.2); the Canaanite king attacking (the foe in the south) — the assault (the enemy's onset); 'he fought against Israel, and took some of them prisoners' (the assault and the captives taken, the trial of the way, the figure, the foe assailing the pilgrim people, the providence, the assault, the vow of Israel to follow, vv.2-3) — Henry, Gill.

themes: And when king Arad the Canaanite, which dwelt in the south, heard tell that Israel came by the way of the spies; then he fought against Israel, and took some of them prisoners

schools.summary: Israel's vow to devote the Canaanite cities to the LORD: 'and Israel vowed a vow unto the LORD, and said, If thou wilt indeed deliver this people into my hand, then I will utterly destroy their cities' — Israel vowed that, if God gave the Canaanites into their hand, they would devote their cities to destruction (the Israel vowed a vow unto the LORD, the vow made to God, the if thou wilt indeed deliver this people into my hand, the appeal for victory, the then I will utterly destroy their cities, the vow to devote the cities, the herem, the figure, the people turning to God in the trial, vowing the spoil to him, the turn from the murmuring of the past to the prayer and vow of faith, the truth that the people of God appeal to him in the day of battle, devoting the victory to him, the figure of the vow of faith, the appeal to God who gives the victory, the spoil consecrated to him, the contrast with the unbelief of the spies, here faith vowing to God); Israel vowing to God (the vow in the trial) — the vow (the appeal to God); 'I will utterly destroy their cities' (the vow to devote the cities, the herem, the spoil to God, the figure, the appeal of faith, the victory devoted to God, the providence, the vow, the victory of Hormah to follow, vv.3) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And Israel vowed a vow unto the LORD, and said, If thou wilt indeed deliver this people into my hand, then I will utterly destroy their cities

schools.summary: The LORD giving the victory and the naming of Hormah: 'and the LORD hearkened to the voice of Israel, and delivered up the Canaanites; and they utterly destroyed them and their cities: and he called the name of the place Hormah' — God answered the vow, gave the Canaanites into Israel's hand, and the place was named Hormah, 'utter destruction' (the the LORD hearkened to the voice of Israel, the vow heard, the prayer answered, the delivered up the Canaanites; and they utterly destroyed them and their cities, the victory and the devoting, the he called the name of the place Hormah, the name, 'devotion to destruction,' the figure, the victory given in answer to the vow of faith, the first conquest near the land, the truth that God hears the appeal of his people and gives the victory, the figure of faith rewarded, the foe delivered up, the place named for the victory, the contrast with the earlier defeat at Hormah, Numbers 14:45, now the place of victory, faith reversing the fruit of unbelief); God hearing the vow and giving the victory (the prayer answered) — the victory (the foe delivered up); 'he called the name of the place Hormah' (the devoting and the name, Hormah, the utter destruction, Numbers 14:45, the figure, faith reversing the earlier defeat, the providence, the victory, the journey from Hor to follow, vv.4) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

schools.summary: The people discouraged by the long way around Edom: 'and they journeyed from mount Hor by the way of the Red sea, to compass the land of Edom: and the soul of the people was much discouraged because of the way' — Israel, turning back to compass Edom, grew discouraged by the long, hard way (the journeyed from mount Hor by the way of the Red sea, the route, southward, the to compass the land of Edom, the long way round, since Edom refused passage, the the soul of the people was much discouraged because of the way, the discouragement, the weariness of the long road, the figure, the trial of the long way, the discouragement that tempts to murmuring, the people wearied by the hard road, the way longer for Edom's refusal, the truth that the way of God's people is often long and trying, the heart tempted to faint, the figure of the discouragement of the pilgrimage, the soul cast down by the way, the temptation to murmur against God, vv.5); the long way round Edom (the hard road) — the way (the long route); 'the soul of the people was much discouraged because of the way' (the weariness of the long road, the discouragement, the figure, the soul cast down by the way, the providence, the discouragement, the murmuring to follow, vv.5) — Henry, Gill.

themes: And they journeyed from mount Hor by the way of the Red sea, to compass the land of Edom: and the soul of the people was much discouraged because of the way

schools.summary: The people's murmuring and loathing of the manna: 'and the people spake against God, and against Moses, Wherefore have ye brought us up out of Egypt to die in the wilderness? for there is no bread, neither is there any water; and our soul loatheth this light bread' — the loathing — 'the people spake against God, and against Moses... our soul loatheth this light bread': discouraged by the way, the people spoke against God and Moses, complaining of no bread and water, and despising the manna as worthless ('light') bread (the witnesses noting the significance — the despising of God's provision, the manna, the bread of heaven God gave them daily, now loathed as worthless, the gift of God scorned, the lesson — the sin of contempt for God's gracious provision, the people calling the heavenly bread 'this light bread,' loathing what God gave, the ingratitude that despises the gift, 'they despised the pleasant land... but murmured in their tents,' Psalm 106:24-25, 'man did eat angels' food,' Psalm 78:25, yet they loathed it, the aggravation — speaking against God himself, charging him with bringing them out to die, the figure — the danger of loathing the heavenly bread, the warning against despising the provision of God, the type of those who despise Christ the true bread from heaven, 'I am the living bread which came down from heaven,' John 6:48-51, the contempt for the manna a figure of the contempt for Christ, the bread of life loathed by the unbelieving, the truth that the sin against God's provision brought the fiery serpents, vv.6, the figure of the ingratitude that despises God's gifts and provokes his judgment, the figure of the loathing of the bread of heaven and the despising of God's grace); the people speaking against God and Moses, charging them with bringing them out to die (the murmuring, the ingratitude, Psalm 106:24-25) — the murmuring (against God); the people loathing the manna as worthless bread (the despising of God's provision, Psalm 78:25; John 6:48) — the figure (the contempt for the bread of heaven, the type of those who despise Christ, the providence, the loathing, the fiery serpents to follow, vv.6) — Calvin, Keil, Ellicott, Wesley, Gill.

debates.question: What does the loathing of the manna reveal?

sides.position: The despising of God's gracious provision, the bread of heaven scorned as worthless 'light bread' (Psalm 106:24-25; 78:25) - the ingratitude that provokes God's judgment, a figure of the contempt for Christ the true bread from heaven (John 6:48-51)

themes: And the people spake against God, and against Moses, Wherefore have ye brought us up out of Egypt to die in the wilderness? for there is no bread, neither is there any water; and our soul loatheth this light bread

a figure of the contempt for Christ the true bread from heaven (John 6:48-51)

schools.summary: The fiery serpents sent in judgment: 'and the LORD sent fiery serpents among the people, and they bit the people; and much people of Israel died' — God sent venomous ('fiery') serpents among them, whose bite was deadly, and many died (the the LORD sent fiery serpents among the people, the judgment, the serpents sent, the and they bit the people; and much people of Israel died, the deadly bite, the death, the figure, the judgment on the murmuring, the serpents the instrument of God's wrath, the 'fiery' serpents, so called for the burning of their venom, the truth that the sin against God's provision brought a deadly judgment, the people perishing by the serpents, the figure of the venom of sin, the deadly bite, the wages of sin death, Romans 6:23, the serpent, the old enemy, the instrument of judgment, that drove the people to repentance and to the remedy God would provide, vv.7-9); God sending the fiery serpents (the judgment) — the judgment (the serpents sent); 'they bit the people; and much people of Israel died' (the deadly bite and the death, the wages of sin, Romans 6:23, the figure, the venom of sin, the deadly bite, the providence, the judgment, the people's confession to follow, vv.7) — Keil and Delitzsch, Gill.

themes: And the LORD sent fiery serpents among the people, and they bit the people; and much people of Israel died

schools.summary: The people's confession and Moses's prayer: 'therefore the people came to Moses, and said, We have sinned, for we have spoken against the LORD, and against thee; pray unto the LORD, that he take away the serpents from us. And Moses prayed for the people' — stricken by the serpents, the people confessed their sin and begged Moses to pray; and Moses prayed (the We have sinned, for we have spoken against the LORD, and against thee, the confession of the sin, the pray unto the LORD, that he take away the serpents, the plea for relief, the And Moses prayed for the people, the intercession, the figure, the judgment driving the people to repentance and confession, the sinner owning the sin and seeking the mediator's prayer, the truth that the affliction brings the sinner to confess and to seek God, the figure of the meek Moses again interceding for the people who wronged him, the mediator praying, a figure of Christ who intercedes for sinners, the confession that prepares for the remedy God will give, vv.8-9); the people confessing their sin (the repentance) — the confession (the sin owned); 'pray unto the LORD... And Moses prayed for the people' (the plea and Moses's prayer, the mediator interceding, the figure, Christ interceding for sinners, the providence, the prayer, the remedy of the brazen serpent to follow, vv.8-9) — Gill.

schools.summary: The command to make the serpent and the promise that the bitten who look shall live: 'and the LORD said unto Moses, Make thee a fiery serpent, and set it upon a pole: and it shall come to pass, that every one that is bitten, when he looketh upon it, shall live' — the look that lives — 'make thee a fiery serpent, and set it upon a pole... every one that is bitten, when he looketh upon it, shall live': God commanded Moses to make a serpent of brass and set it on a pole, that the bitten might look on it and live (the witnesses noting the significance — the remedy by a look, the serpent lifted up, and life given to all who looked, the simplest act of faith, the lesson — the figure of the look of faith to the lifted-up Christ, the serpent of brass, in the likeness of the serpents that bit them, lifted up, that the dying might look and live, the very image of the curse made the means of healing, the truth that the remedy was not in the looker but in the appointed object, the bitten healed simply by looking, the great fulfillment — Christ himself making this the figure of his cross: 'as Moses lifted up the serpent in the wilderness, even so must the Son of man be lifted up: that whosoever believeth in him should not perish, but have eternal life,' John 3:14-15, the serpent of brass, made in the likeness of sinful flesh yet without venom, a figure of Christ 'made sin for us,' 2 Corinthians 5:21, 'in the likeness of sinful flesh,' Romans 8:3, lifted up on the cross, the look the figure of faith, 'look unto me, and be ye saved,' Isaiah 45:22, the truth that the dying sinner, bitten by the old serpent, lives by looking in faith to the lifted-up Christ, the figure of the universal remedy, every one that looketh, none excluded who look, the figure of the look of faith to the lifted-up Saviour); the serpent of brass set on a pole, lifted up (the remedy appointed, the figure of the cross, John 3:14) — the lifting up (the serpent on the pole); the bitten healed by looking, the look of faith (the remedy by a look, the figure of believing on Christ, Isaiah 45:22; John 3:15) — the figure (Christ lifted up, the look of faith, the providence, the command, the making of the serpent to follow, vv.9) — Calvin, Keil, Ellicott, Wesley, Gill.

debates.question: What does the bronze serpent lifted up signify?

sides.position: The remedy by a look, the bitten healed simply by looking on the appointed object - a figure (which Christ made of his cross) of the look of faith to the lifted-up Son of man, that whosoever believeth should not perish (John 3:14-15), the serpent in the likeness of the curse a figure of Christ made sin for us (2 Corinthians 5:21; Romans 8:3; Isaiah 45:22)

themes: And the LORD said unto Moses, Make thee a fiery serpent, and set it upon a pole: and it shall come to pass, that every one that is bitten, when he looketh upon it, shall live

the remedy by a look, the bitten healed simply by looking on the appointed object
a figure of the look of faith to the lifted-up Christ, that whosoever believeth should not perish (John 3:14-15; Isaiah 45:22; 2 Corinthians 5:21)

schools.summary: The brazen serpent made and the bitten who beheld it living: 'and Moses made a serpent of brass, and put it upon a pole, and it came to pass, that if a serpent had bitten any man, when he beheld the serpent of brass, he lived' — the beholding that lives — 'Moses made a serpent of brass, and put it upon a pole... when he beheld the serpent of brass, he lived': Moses made the serpent of brass and set it up, and every bitten man who beheld it was healed and lived (the witnesses noting the significance — the healing by beholding, not by touch, nor medicine, nor merit, but simply by looking on the lifted-up serpent the dying lived, the lesson — the salvation of the sinner by faith's look to the crucified Christ, the bitten healed by beholding, a figure of the soul stung by sin saved by looking to Christ lifted up, the remedy free, present, and sufficient for all who look, the truth that there was healing in a look, 'there is life in a look at the Crucified One,' the look of faith the only condition, the fulfillment — 'even so must the Son of man be lifted up,' John 3:14, the serpent a figure of Christ lifted up on the cross, the beholding a figure of faith, the universal sufficiency — 'any man' who beheld lived, none who looked excluded, a figure of 'whosoever believeth,' John 3:15-16, the later abuse — the serpent kept and worshipped, called Nehushtan, broken by Hezekiah, 2 Kings 18:4, a warning against turning the means into an idol, the figure of the simple faith that beholds Christ and lives, the gospel remedy for the deadly sting of sin, the figure of life by beholding the lifted-up Saviour); Moses making the serpent of brass and lifting it up (the remedy set up, the figure of the cross, John 3:14) — the making (the serpent lifted up); the bitten man healed by beholding, none excluded who looked (the salvation by faith's look, John 3:15-16; 2 Kings 18:4) — the figure (the look of faith to Christ crucified, the providence, the healing, the journey resumed to follow, vv.10) — Calvin, Keil, Ellicott, Wesley, Gill.

debates.question: What does the bitten man's healing by beholding teach?

sides.position: The dying healed simply by looking on the lifted-up serpent, not by merit or medicine - a figure of the soul stung by sin saved by faith's look to the crucified Christ (John 3:14-16), the universal 'any man' answering to 'whosoever believeth,' with the later worship of the serpent as Nehushtan a warning against idolizing the means (2 Kings 18:4)

themes: And Moses made a serpent of brass, and put it upon a pole, and it came to pass, that if a serpent had bitten any man, when he beheld the serpent of brass, he lived
the brazen serpent made and the bitten who beheld it living (the crux of healing by beholding the lifted-up Christ)
the dying healed not by merit or medicine but simply by looking on the lifted-up serpent
a figure of the soul saved by faith's look to the lifted-up Christ, none excluded who look (John 3:14-16; 2 Kings 18:4)

schools.summary: The camp beyond Arnon, the border of Moab: 'from thence they removed, and pitched on the other side of Arnon, which is in the wilderness that cometh out of the coasts of the Amorites: for Arnon is the border of Moab, between Moab and the Amorites' — Israel encamped beyond the Arnon, the boundary between Moab and the Amorites (the pitched on the other side of Arnon, the camp beyond the Arnon, the Arnon is the border of Moab, between Moab and the Amorites, the boundary, the figure, the journey bringing Israel to the border of the Amorites, the foe whose land they would take, the way marked by the rivers and borders, the pilgrimage traced, the truth that God led his people stage by stage to the very border of conquest, the figure of the approach to the inheritance, the border crossed, the land of the Amorites at hand, Deuteronomy 2:24); the camp beyond the Arnon (the stage) — the camp (beyond Arnon); 'Arnon is the border of Moab, between Moab and the Amorites' (the boundary between Moab and the Amorites, the border, Deuteronomy 2:24, the figure, the approach to the conquest, the providence, the border reached, the song of the well to follow, vv.14-18) — Clarke, Gill.

schools.summary: The well of Beer where God gave water: 'and from thence they went to Beer: that is the well whereof the LORD spake unto Moses, Gather the people together, and I will give them water' — at Beer ('well') God promised to give the people water, bidding Moses gather them (the from thence they went to Beer, the place, the well, the the well whereof the LORD spake unto Moses, the well God promised, the Gather the people together, and I will give them water, the promise of water, the figure, God giving water in the wilderness, the provision for his people, the contrast with the earlier murmuring for water, now God giving it freely, without their striving, the truth that God provides for his people's need, the water given at his word, the figure of the water of life freely given, the well of salvation, 'with joy shall ye draw water out of the wells of salvation,' Isaiah 12:3, the gift of water answered by the song of the well, vv.17); the well of Beer that God promised (the provision) — the well (God's gift); 'Gather the people together, and I will give them water' (the promise of water, God's provision, Isaiah 12:3, the figure, the water of life freely given, the providence, the water given, the song of the well to follow, vv.17-18) — Calvin, Gill.

schools.summary: The song of the well, Israel's joyful praise for the water: 'then Israel sang this song, Spring up, O well; sing ye unto it' — the song of joy — 'then Israel sang this song, Spring up, O well; sing ye unto it': when God gave them water, Israel sang a song of joy and praise, calling on the well to spring up (the witnesses noting the significance — the song of thanksgiving for God's provision, the people, so often murmuring, now singing for the gift of water, joy and praise instead of complaint, the lesson — the praise of faith for the gifts of God, the well sung over, the water received with thanksgiving, the contrast with the murmuring at the loathing of the manna, vv.5, the right response to God's provision is the song of praise, the figure — the water of life and the song of the redeemed, 'with joy shall ye draw water out of the wells of salvation,' Isaiah 12:3, the well a figure of the springs of grace, the water of life that Christ gives, 'the water that I shall give him shall be in him a well of water springing up into everlasting life,' John 4:14, 'if any man thirst, let him come unto me, and drink,' John 7:37, the song of the well a figure of the joy of those who drink of the living water and praise the Giver, the truth that God's people sing over his provision, the well of salvation drawn with joy, the figure of the praise of the redeemed for the water of life); Israel singing for the gift of water (the song of joy and thanksgiving, the contrast with the murmuring) — the song (the praise for the water); 'Spring up, O well; sing ye unto it' (the call on the well to spring up, the joyful praise, the song of faith over God's provision, Isaiah 12:3; John 4:14, the figure, the water of life, the wells of salvation, the providence, the song, the princes who dug the well to follow, vv.18) — Calvin, Keil, Clarke, Wesley, Gill.

debates.question: What does the song of the well signify?

sides.position: The praise of thanksgiving for God's provision, the people singing over the gift of water in striking contrast with their murmuring - a figure of the joy of the redeemed who draw water from the wells of salvation (Isaiah 12:3) and the living water Christ gives, springing up into everlasting life (John 4:14; 7:37)

schools.summary: God's word of assurance against Og, 'fear him not': 'and the LORD said unto Moses, Fear him not: for I have delivered him into thy hand, and all his people, and his land; and thou shalt do to him as thou didst unto Sihon king of the Amorites, which dwelt at Heshbon' — the word of faith — 'fear him not: for I have delivered him into thy hand, and all his people, and his land': against Og the giant, God bid Moses not to fear, for he had already given Og into Israel's hand (the witnesses noting the significance — God's assurance against the mightiest foe, Og the giant king, of the remnant of the giants, the most terrible of enemies, yet given into Israel's hand before the battle, the lesson — the victory of faith resting on God's promise, the same giants the spies had feared, Numbers 13:33, now faced with faith, the foe delivered up by God's word, 'fear not' the word of God to his people in the face of the enemy, the assurance that turns the battle, 'I have delivered him into thy hand' — the victory given before it is won, the contrast with the unbelief of Kadesh, where the giants were feared, the figure — the believer's victory over the greatest foe by faith in God's word, 'if God be for us, who can be against us?'
